disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Progressive intermittent dysphagia to dry solids (bread, meat) over the preceding 6 months; tonight is the first frank bolus impaction
trigger_question_examples: Have you had any trouble swallowing food before tonight?
Does food often feel like it goes down slowly or catches, especially dry foods like bread or steak?
Is this the first time food has actually got stuck?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Now that you ask, yeah — for the last six months or so dry things like bread and steak sometimes feel like they go down slowly or catch for a second. But it's never actually stuck like this before.
disclosure_items.patient_deflection_template: I mean, I hadn't really thought about it before tonight.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Cannot swallow own saliva; continuously spitting out frothy saliva since the food lodged (complete oesophageal obstruction with aspiration risk)
trigger_question_examples: Can you swallow at all right now — even your own saliva or a sip of water?
What happens when you try to swallow?
Are you able to keep any fluids down?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No — I can't swallow anything, not even my own spit. It just sits there or comes back up, so I keep spitting into a bowl.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Retrosternal chest pressure/pain accompanying the impaction
trigger_question_examples: Tell me about the pain — where exactly is it and what does it feel like?
Do you have any chest pain along with the stuck feeling?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: There's a pressure kind of pain right behind my breastbone, in the middle of my chest, where the food feels stuck.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No stridor, no shortness of breath, voice normal — airway patent (pertinent negative)
trigger_question_examples: Are you having any trouble breathing, or is your breathing noisy?
Has your voice changed, or do you feel like your airway is affected?
Are you coughing or feeling like saliva is going down the wrong way?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No, my breathing is fine and my voice is normal. It's not in my windpipe — it's lower down, and I'm not coughing.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Pain is clearly triggered by swallowing rather than exertion; no radiation to arm or jaw, no sweating (features that argue against acute coronary syndrome)
trigger_question_examples: Does the pain spread anywhere — into your arm, neck or jaw?
Are you sweaty or clammy, or does the pain come on with exertion?
Did the pain start with the food getting stuck, or on its own?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: It started exactly when the food got stuck, and it's worse when I try to swallow. It doesn't spread to my arm or jaw, and I'm not sweaty — I'm just anxious.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No haematemesis, no sudden severe or tearing chest/back pain, no pain worse on breathing (pertinent negatives against oesophageal perforation / Boerhaave)
trigger_question_examples: Did you get a sudden very severe or tearing pain in your chest or back?
Is the pain worse when you take a deep breath?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No blood — just the frothy saliva. There was no sudden tearing pain, and breathing in doesn't make it worse. It's the swallowing that's the problem.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Atopic background — childhood asthma and eczema
trigger_question_examples: Do you have any allergies, asthma or eczema?
Any history of allergic conditions in yourself?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yeah, I've had asthma and eczema since I was a kid.
